brief: GAP/WHY: Conventional heart rate variability (HRV) metrics correlate only modestly with sudden cardiac death (SCD); better risk stratification needed. Detrended fluctuation analysis (DFA) with new methods to assess short-term scaling exponent may outperform conventional HRV. | OBJECTIVE: Test predictive value of ultra-short-term heart rate fluctuations via DFA for incident SCD. | DESIGN: Prospective cohort, FINCAVAS (Finnish Cardiovascular Study); participants undergoing clinical exercise testing. | POPULATION/N: 2,794 participants. | EXPOSURE/MEASURE: Ultra-short-term heart rate fluctuations from 1-minute ECG samples analyzed by DFA, at rest and during different stages of physical exertion. Main exposure variable = short-scale scaling exponent computed with second-order detrending (DFA2 alpha1). | OUTCOME DEFINITION: SCD defined by AHA/ESC criteria using death certificates with written event accounts. | FOLLOW-UP: median 8.3 years (Q1-Q3: 6.4-10.5). | KEY RESULTS: 83 SCDs occurred during follow-up. DFA2 alpha1 measured AT REST (but NOT during exercise) associated highly significantly with SCD risk: 1-SD lower value = 2.4-fold risk (Q1-Q3: 2.0-3.0), P<0.001. Association persisted after adjusting for major SCD risk factors (age, cardiovascular morbidities, cardiorespiratory fitness, heart rate reduction, LV ejection fraction). Conventional HRV parameters (at any exercise stage or at rest) showed substantially weaker associations with SCD and were statistically nonsignificant after adjustment for other risk factors. | TAKEAWAY: Ultra-short-term DFA2 alpha1 measured at rest is a powerful, independent predictor of SCD; the DFA2 alpha1-SCD association is modified by physical exertion (resting measurement is the predictive one).
